turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show! Today we're diving into clinical trial design — the architecture behind how we know if a treatment actually works. I'm thrilled to have you here to help us understand what makes a great trial.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Thanks for having me. Think of trial design like the foundation of a building—get it wrong, everything crumbles. The randomized controlled trial is our gold standard because randomization distributes both known and unknown confounders equally, letting us confidently attribute outcomes to the treatment itself.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That building foundation analogy really works. So once we've got participants randomized into groups, how do we actually measure success? What goes into choosing the right endpoints?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: The primary endpoint is your bullseye—it must be clinically meaningful and directly relevant to your therapeutic claim. In oncology, that's overall survival; in diabetes, it's hemoglobin A1c reduction. Pick the wrong target, and even a perfectly run trial gives you an answer to a question nobody cares about.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: critical point, and it gets trickier with surrogate endpoints—like using blood pressure as a stand-in for stroke prevention. They can speed up drug development dramatically, but only when that surrogate-to-clinical-outcome relationship is properly validated. Otherwise, you're betting on a proxy that might not deliver.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's a really important caution about surrogates. As we wrap up, what would you say is the single most common pitfall in trial design that keeps you up at night?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Underpowered trials—when sponsors overestimate the treatment effect to keep sample sizes small and costs low. You end up unable to detect a real benefit even if one exists. It's like bringing a magnifying glass to find a needle in a haystack—you're almost guaranteed to miss it.
